docs.title: ExpMate
docs.text:
ML Research Boilerplate — Config & Logging First

Welcome to ExpMate, a lightweight experiment management toolkit designed for ML researchers who want to focus on their experiments, not on boilerplate code.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: ExpMate provides clean, reusable patterns for configuration management, logging, and experiment tracking—everything you need to run reproducible ML experiments.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• 🔧 Configuration Management: YAML-based configs with command-line overrides
  • 📊 Experiment Logging: Structured logging with metrics tracking
  • 🚀 PyTorch Integration: Checkpoint management and DDP utilities
  • 📈 Experiment Tracking: Built-in support for WandB and TensorBoard
  • 🔍 CLI Tools: Compare runs, visualize metrics, and manage sweeps
  • 🔄 Git Integration: Automatic git info tracking for reproducibility

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
from expmate import ExperimentLogger, parse_config, set_seed

# Parse config from YAML + command-line overrides
config = parse_config()

# Set random seed for reproducibility
set_seed(config.seed)

# Create experiment logger
logger = ExperimentLogger(run_dir=f"runs/{config.run_id}")
logger.info(f"Starting experiment: {config.run_id}")

# Your training code here...
for epoch in range(config.training.epochs):
    # ... training logic ...

    # Log metrics
    logger.log_metric(step=epoch, split='train', name='loss', value=loss)
    logger.info(f"Epoch {epoch}: loss={loss:.4f}")

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
pip install expmate

For additional features:

# PyTorch support
pip install expmate[torch]

# Experiment tracking (WandB, TensorBoard)
pip install expmate[tracking]

# Visualization tools
pip install expmate[viz]

# Everything
pip install expmate[all]

docs.title: Changelog
docs.text:
All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file.

The format is based on Keep a Changelog, and this project adheres to Semantic Versioning.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Documentation site with MkDocs
  • Comprehensive README for PyPI

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Initial release
  • Configuration management with YAML and CLI overrides
  • Experiment logging with structured metrics
  • Checkpoint management for PyTorch models
  • Distributed training utilities (DDP support)
  • WandB and TensorBoard integration
  • CLI tools for comparing runs and visualizing metrics
  • Git integration for reproducibility
  • Hyperparameter sweep utilities
  • Examples and documentation

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Hierarchical configuration with dot notation
  • Variable interpolation in configs
  • Rank-aware logging for distributed training
  • Best model tracking
  • Automatic checkpoint cleanup
  • DDP-safe run directory creation
  • Metrics visualization
  • Run comparison tools

docs.title: Contributing to ExpMate
docs.text: Thank you for your interest in contributing to ExpMate! This guide will help you get started.

docs.title: 1. Fork and Clone
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/expmate.git
cd expmate

docs.title: 2. Install Development Dependencies
docs.text:
pip install -e ".[dev]"

This installs ExpMate in editable mode along with development tools: - pytest: Testing framework - pytest-cov: Coverage reporting - black: Code formatting - ruff: Linting - mypy: Type checking - pre-commit: Git hooks

docs.title: 3. Install Pre-commit Hooks
docs.text:
pre-commit install

This sets up automatic code formatting and linting on commit.

docs.title: 1. Create a Branch
docs.text:
git checkout -b feature/your-feature-name

Use descriptive branch names: - feature/add-new-parser - fix/checkpoint-bug - docs/update-readme

docs.title: 2. Make Your Changes
docs.text: Write clean, well-documented code following the existing style.

docs.title: 3. Write Tests
docs.text:
Add tests for your changes in the tests/ directory:

# tests/test_your_feature.py
import pytest
from expmate import YourFeature

def test_your_feature():
    feature = YourFeature()
    assert feature.works() == True

docs.title: 4. Run Tests
docs.text:
# Run all tests
pytest

# Run with coverage
pytest --cov=expmate

# Run specific test file
pytest tests/test_config.py

# Run specific test
pytest tests/test_config.py::test_load_config

docs.title: 5. Format Code
docs.text:
# Format with black
black src/expmate tests

# Lint with ruff
ruff check src/expmate tests

# Type check with mypy
mypy src/expmate

docs.title: 6. Commit Changes
docs.text:
git add .
git commit -m "Add feature: descriptive message"

Pre-commit hooks will automatically format and lint your code.

docs.title: 7. Push and Create PR
docs.text:
git push origin feature/your-feature-name

Then create a Pull Request on GitHub.

docs.title: Python Style Guide
docs.text:
• Follow PEP 8
  • Use black for formatting (line length: 88)
  • Use type hints for function signatures
  • Write docstrings in Google style

Example:

def load_config(
    config_input: Union[str, List[str], Dict[str, Any]], 
    overrides: List[str] = None
) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Load and merge configuration from YAML files or dict.

    Args:
        config_input: Config file path(s) or dict
        overrides: List of key=value overrides

    Returns:
        Loaded and merged configuration

    Raises:
        FileNotFoundError: If config file doesn't exist
        ValueError: If config format is invalid
    """
    pass

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• Add docstrings to all public functions and classes
  • Include examples in docstrings when helpful
  • Update relevant documentation in docs/

docs.title: Test Coverage
docs.text:
• Aim for >80% test coverage
  • Test both success and failure cases
  • Include edge cases

docs.title: Test Organization
docs.text:
tests/
├── test_config.py      # Config management tests
├── test_logger.py      # Logging tests
├── test_checkpoint.py  # Checkpoint tests
└── test_integration.py # Integration tests

docs.title: Test Naming
docs.text:
Use descriptive test names:

def test_load_config_from_yaml_file():
    """Test loading config from YAML file."""
    pass

def test_load_config_with_overrides():
    """Test loading config with CLI overrides."""
    pass

def test_load_config_raises_on_missing_file():
    """Test that load_config raises FileNotFoundError."""
    pass

docs.title: Building Docs Locally
docs.text:
# Install docs dependencies
pip install mkdocs mkdocs-material mkdocstrings[python]

# Serve docs locally
mkdocs serve

# Build docs
mkdocs build

Visit http://localhost:8000 to view documentation.

docs.title: PR Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] Tests pass (pytest)
  • [ ] Code is formatted (black)
  • [ ] Code is linted (ruff)
  • [ ] Type hints are correct (mypy)
  • [ ] Documentation is updated
  • [ ] CHANGELOG.md is updated
  • [ ] Commit messages are descriptive

docs.title: PR Description
docs.text:
Include in your PR description: - What changes you made - Why you made them - How to test them - Any breaking changes

Example:

## Description
Add support for remote config files via HTTP/HTTPS URLs.

## Motivation
Users requested the ability to load configs from remote locations.

## Changes
- Add `load_config_from_url()` function
- Update `load_config()` to detect and handle URLs
- Add tests for URL loading
- Update documentation

## Testing
```bash
pytest tests/test_config.py::test_load_config_from_url

docs.title: Breaking Changes
docs.text: None ```

docs.title: Release Process
docs.text:
Releases are managed by maintainers:

  1. Update version in pyproject.toml and src/expmate/__init__.py
  2. Update CHANGELOG.md
  3. Create git tag: git tag v0.2.0
  4. Push tag: git push origin v0.2.0
  5. Build and publish to PyPI

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:

  • Bugs: Open a GitHub Issue

docs.text: Be respectful and inclusive. We follow the Contributor Covenant.

docs.title: CheckpointManager
docs.text:
Manages model checkpoints with automatic cleanup and best model tracking.

from expmate.torch import CheckpointManager

manager = CheckpointManager(
    checkpoint_dir='checkpoints',
    keep_last=3,
    keep_best=5,
    metric_name='val_loss',
    mode='min'
)

docs.title: __init__(checkpoint_dir, keep_last=3, keep_best=3, metric_name='loss', mode='min')
docs.text:
Initialize checkpoint manager.

Parameters: - checkpoint_dir (str): Directory to save checkpoints - keep_last (int): Number of last checkpoints to keep - keep_best (int): Number of best checkpoints to keep - metric_name (str): Metric name for tracking best checkpoints - mode (str): 'min' or 'max' - whether lower or higher is better

docs.title: save(model, optimizer=None, scheduler=None, epoch=None, step=None, metrics=None, extra=None, filename=None)
docs.text:
Save a checkpoint.

Returns: Path to saved checkpoint file

docs.title: load_latest()
docs.text:
Load the most recent checkpoint.

Returns: Checkpoint dictionary

docs.title: load_best()
docs.text:
Load the best checkpoint based on tracked metric.

Returns: Checkpoint dictionary

See the source code for full API details.

docs.title: Config Class
docs.text:
A dictionary-like configuration object with attribute access.

from expmate import Config

config = Config({'model': {'hidden_dim': 256}})
print(config.model.hidden_dim)  # 256

docs.title: load_config()
docs.text:
Load configuration from YAML files or dictionaries.

from expmate.config import load_config

config = load_config('config.yaml')
config = load_config('config.yaml', overrides=['training.lr=0.01'])

Parameters: - config_input (str|list|dict): Config file path(s) or dict - overrides (list): List of key=value overrides

Returns: Dictionary with configuration

See the source code for full API details.

docs.title: ExperimentLogger
docs.text:
Structured logging and metrics tracking for experiments.

from expmate import ExperimentLogger

logger = ExperimentLogger(run_dir='runs/exp1')
logger.info("Training started")
logger.log_metric(step=0, split='train', name='loss', value=0.5)

docs.title: __init__(run_dir, rank=0, run_id=None, log_level='INFO', console_output=True)
docs.text: Initialize experiment logger.

docs.title: info(message), warning(message), error(message), debug(message)
docs.text: Log text messages at different levels.

docs.title: log_metric(step, split, name, value)
docs.text:
Log a metric value.

Parameters: - step (int): Training step/epoch - split (str): Data split ('train', 'val', 'test') - name (str): Metric name - value (float): Metric value

docs.title: track_best(metric_name, mode='min')
docs.text:
Track best value for a metric.

Parameters: - metric_name (str): Name of metric to track - mode (str): 'min' or 'max'

docs.title: profile(name)
docs.text:
Context manager for profiling code sections.

with logger.profile('data_loading'):
    data = load_data()

See the source code for full API details.

docs.title: parse_config()
docs.text:
Parse configuration from command-line arguments.

from expmate import parse_config

# Automatically parses: python script.py config.yaml key=value
config = parse_config()

docs.title: ConfigArgumentParser
docs.text:
Argument parser with config support.

from expmate import ConfigArgumentParser

parser = ConfigArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument('--gpu', type=int, default=0)
config = parser.parse_config()

docs.title: parse_value()
docs.text:
Parse string value to appropriate type.

from expmate.parser import parse_value

parse_value('42')      # 42 (int)
parse_value('3.14')    # 3.14 (float)
parse_value('true')    # True (bool)
parse_value('[1,2,3]') # [1, 2, 3] (list)

See the source code for full API details.

docs.title: WandB Tracker
docs.text:
Integration with Weights & Biases.

from expmate.tracking import WandbTracker

tracker = WandbTracker(
    project="my-project",
    name="exp1",
    config=config.to_dict()
)

tracker.log({'train/loss': loss}, step=epoch)
tracker.finish()

docs.title: DDP Training Example
docs.text:
(Documentation in progress - will be added in next update)

See the full example: examples/train.py

docs.title: Minimal Example
docs.text: This example demonstrates the simplest way to use ExpMate.

docs.title: Source Code
docs.text: The complete example is available at examples/minimal.py.

docs.title: Configuration File
docs.text:
Create conf/example.yaml:

run_id: "exp_${now:%Y%m%d_%H%M%S}"
seed: 42

model:
  input_dim: 128
  output_dim: 10

training:
  epochs: 5
  lr: 0.001

docs.title: Training Script
docs.text:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, TensorDataset

from expmate import ExperimentLogger, parse_config, set_seed


def main():
    # Parse config from command line
    config = parse_config()

    # Set random seed for reproducibility
    set_seed(int(config.seed))

    # Create experiment logger
    logger = ExperimentLogger(run_dir=f"runs/{config.run_id}")
    logger.info(f"Starting experiment: {config.run_id}")
    logger.info(f"Config: seed={config.seed}, lr={config.training.lr}")

    # Create dummy model and data
    model = nn.Linear(int(config.model.input_dim), int(config.model.output_dim))
    dataset = TensorDataset(
        torch.randn(100, int(config.model.input_dim)),
        torch.randint(0, int(config.model.output_dim), (100,)),
    )
    dataloader = DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=32)

    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=float(config.training.lr))
    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

    # Training loop
    for epoch in range(int(config.training.epochs)):
        total_loss = 0

        for batch_x, batch_y in dataloader:
            output = model(batch_x)
            loss = criterion(output, batch_y)
            loss.backward()
            optimizer.step()
            optimizer.zero_grad()

            total_loss += loss.item()

        avg_loss = total_loss / len(dataloader)

        # Log metrics
        logger.log_metric(step=epoch, split="train", name="loss", value=avg_loss)
        logger.info(f"Epoch {epoch}/{config.training.epochs}: loss={avg_loss:.4f}")

    logger.info("Training complete!")
    logger.info(f"Logs saved to: {logger.run_dir}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

docs.title: Basic Run
docs.text: python examples/minimal.py examples/conf/example.yaml

docs.title: With Parameter Overrides
docs.text:
# Change learning rate
python examples/minimal.py examples/conf/example.yaml training.lr=0.01

# Change multiple parameters
python examples/minimal.py examples/conf/example.yaml training.lr=0.01 training.epochs=10

# Add new parameters
python examples/minimal.py examples/conf/example.yaml +optimizer.weight_decay=0.0001

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
ExpMate creates a structured run directory:

runs/
└── exp_20250123_143022/
    ├── run.yaml          # Saved configuration
    ├── exp.log           # Training logs
    ├── events.jsonl      # Structured events
    ├── metrics.csv       # Training metrics
    └── best.json         # Best metric values

docs.title: Viewing Logs
docs.text:
# View training logs
cat runs/exp_20250123_143022/exp.log

# View metrics
cat runs/exp_20250123_143022/metrics.csv

docs.title: Comparing Runs
docs.text:
# Run multiple experiments
python examples/minimal.py examples/conf/example.yaml training.lr=0.001
python examples/minimal.py examples/conf/example.yaml training.lr=0.01
python examples/minimal.py examples/conf/example.yaml training.lr=0.1

# Compare results
expmate compare runs/exp_*

docs.text:
1. Configuration Management: Loading and overriding config values
  2. Experiment Logging: Structured logging with automatic file management
  3. Metrics Tracking: Logging and tracking training metrics
  4. Reproducibility: Setting seeds and saving full configuration

docs.title: Basic Concepts
docs.text: This guide explains the core concepts of ExpMate.

docs.title: Configuration Management
docs.text: ExpMate uses a hierarchical configuration system based on YAML files with command-line overrides.

docs.title: Config Object
docs.text:
The Config object provides dictionary-like and attribute-like access:

from expmate import Config

config = Config({
    'model': {'hidden_dim': 256},
    'training': {'lr': 0.001}
})

# Dictionary-style access
print(config['model']['hidden_dim'])  # 256

# Attribute-style access (preferred)
print(config.model.hidden_dim)  # 256
print(config.training.lr)  # 0.001

docs.title: Command-Line Overrides
docs.text:
Override any config value from the command line:

# Simple override
python train.py config.yaml training.lr=0.01

# Nested values
python train.py config.yaml model.hidden_dim=512

# Multiple overrides
python train.py config.yaml training.lr=0.01 training.epochs=50

# Add new keys with + prefix
python train.py config.yaml +optimizer.weight_decay=0.0001

# Type hints for ambiguous values
python train.py config.yaml training.lr:float=1e-3

docs.title: Variable Interpolation
docs.text:
Use variables in your config files:

run_id: "exp_${now:%Y%m%d_%H%M%S}"  # Current timestamp
seed: 42
output_dir: "outputs/${run_id}"     # Reference other values

docs.title: Experiment Logging
docs.text: ExpMate provides structured logging for both text and metrics.

docs.title: Text Logging
docs.text:
Use level-specific methods (preferred):

logger.info("Training started")
logger.warning("Learning rate might be too high")
logger.error("NaN detected in loss")
logger.debug("Batch processing time: 0.5s")

Or the generic method:

logger.log("Training started", level="INFO")

docs.title: Metrics Logging
docs.text:
Log structured metrics for tracking and visualization:

logger.log_metric(
    step=epoch,
    split='train',  # 'train', 'val', 'test', etc.
    name='loss',
    value=0.5
)

All metrics are automatically saved to metrics.csv for easy analysis.

docs.title: Best Metrics Tracking
docs.text:
Track best values for important metrics:

# Configure tracking (mode: 'min' or 'max')
logger.track_best('val_loss', mode='min')
logger.track_best('val_accuracy', mode='max')

# Log metrics normally
logger.log_metric(step=epoch, split='val', name='loss', value=0.5)

# Best values are automatically tracked and saved to best.json

docs.title: Distributed Training
docs.text: ExpMate provides utilities for PyTorch distributed training.

docs.title: Setup DDP
docs.text:
from expmate.torch import mp

# Initialize DDP
rank, local_rank, world_size = mp.setup_ddp()

# Check if main process
is_main = rank == 0

docs.title: Rank-Aware Logging
docs.text:
from expmate import ExperimentLogger

# Pass rank to logger
logger = ExperimentLogger(run_dir=run_dir, rank=rank)

# Only rank 0 writes to main log files
logger.info("This message appears in all rank logs")

docs.title: Shared Run Directory
docs.text:
Create a single run directory shared across all processes:

# DDP-safe directory creation
run_dir = mp.create_shared_run_dir(
    base_dir="runs",
    run_id=config.run_id
)

docs.title: Checkpoint Management
docs.text: Manage model checkpoints with automatic cleanup and best model tracking.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
from expmate.torch import CheckpointManager

manager = CheckpointManager(
    checkpoint_dir='checkpoints',
    keep_last=3,        # Keep last 3 checkpoints
    keep_best=5,        # Keep top 5 checkpoints
    metric_name='val_loss',
    mode='min'          # Lower is better
)

# Save checkpoint
manager.save(
    model=model,
    optimizer=optimizer,
    epoch=epoch,
    metrics={'val_loss': 0.5, 'val_acc': 0.95}
)

# Load latest checkpoint
checkpoint = manager.load_latest()

# Load best checkpoint
best_checkpoint = manager.load_best()

docs.title: Experiment Tracking
docs.text: Integrate with popular tracking tools.

docs.title: Weights & Biases
docs.text:
from expmate.tracking import WandbTracker

tracker = WandbTracker(
    project="my-project",
    name=config.run_id,
    config=config.to_dict()
)

tracker.log({'train/loss': loss}, step=epoch)
tracker.finish()

docs.title: TensorBoard
docs.text:
from expmate.tracking import TensorBoardTracker

tracker = TensorBoardTracker(log_dir=f'runs/{config.run_id}/tb')
tracker.log({'loss': loss}, step=epoch)

docs.title: Run Organization
docs.text:
ExpMate automatically organizes experiments:

runs/
├── exp_20250123_100000/
│   ├── run.yaml          # Config snapshot
│   ├── exp.log           # Human-readable log
│   ├── events.jsonl      # Machine-readable events
│   ├── metrics.csv       # All metrics
│   ├── best.json         # Best metric values
│   └── git_info.json     # Git commit info
├── exp_20250123_110000/
└── exp_20250123_120000/

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text: ExpMate requires Python 3.8 or later.

docs.title: Basic Installation
docs.text:
Install ExpMate using pip:

pip install expmate

This installs the core package with minimal dependencies (PyYAML and NumPy).

docs.title: Optional Dependencies
docs.text: ExpMate provides several optional dependency groups for different use cases:

docs.title: PyTorch Support
docs.text:
For PyTorch-specific features like checkpoint management and DDP utilities:

pip install expmate[torch]

This includes: - torch>=1.12.0

docs.title: Experiment Tracking
docs.text:
For integration with Weights & Biases and TensorBoard:

pip install expmate[tracking]

This includes: - wandb>=0.15.0 - tensorboard>=2.13.0

docs.title: Visualization Tools
docs.text:
For CLI visualization and comparison tools:

pip install expmate[viz]

This includes: - matplotlib>=3.5.0 - polars>=0.20.0

docs.title: System Monitoring
docs.text:
For tracking system resources during training:

pip install expmate[monitor]

This includes: - psutil>=5.9.0

docs.title: Development Tools
docs.text:
For contributing to ExpMate:

pip install expmate[dev]

This includes: - pytest>=7.0.0 - pytest-cov>=4.0.0 - black>=23.0.0 - ruff>=0.1.0 - mypy>=1.0.0 - pre-commit>=3.0.0

docs.title: All Dependencies
docs.text:
To install everything:

pip install expmate[all]

docs.title: Install from Source
docs.text:
To install from source (for development or latest features):

git clone https://github.com/kunheek/expmate.git
cd expmate
pip install -e .

With optional dependencies:

pip install -e ".[all]"

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
Verify the installation by checking the version:

python -c "import expmate; print(expmate.__version__)"

Or use the CLI:

expmate --version

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Quick Start Guide
  • Basic Concepts

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: This guide will walk you through creating your first experiment with ExpMate.

docs.title: Step 1: Create a Configuration File
docs.text:
Create a YAML configuration file config.yaml:

# Run identification
run_id: "exp_${now:%Y%m%d_%H%M%S}"
seed: 42

# Model architecture
model:
  input_dim: 128
  hidden_dim: 256
  output_dim: 10

# Training configuration
training:
  epochs: 10
  lr: 0.001
  batch_size: 32

docs.title: Step 2: Write Your Training Script
docs.text:
Create train.py:

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, TensorDataset

from expmate import ExperimentLogger, parse_config, set_seed

def main():
    # Parse config from command line
    config = parse_config()

    # Set random seed for reproducibility
    set_seed(config.seed)

    # Create experiment logger
    logger = ExperimentLogger(run_dir=f"runs/{config.run_id}")
    logger.info(f"Starting experiment: {config.run_id}")
    logger.info(f"Config: {config.to_dict()}")

    # Create model
    model = nn.Sequential(
        nn.Linear(config.model.input_dim, config.model.hidden_dim),
        nn.ReLU(),
        nn.Linear(config.model.hidden_dim, config.model.output_dim)
    )

    # Create dummy dataset
    dataset = TensorDataset(
        torch.randn(1000, config.model.input_dim),
        torch.randint(0, config.model.output_dim, (1000,))
    )
    dataloader = DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=config.training.batch_size)

    # Setup training
    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=config.training.lr)
    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

    # Training loop
    for epoch in range(config.training.epochs):
        total_loss = 0

        for batch_x, batch_y in dataloader:
            output = model(batch_x)
            loss = criterion(output, batch_y)

            loss.backward()
            optimizer.step()
            optimizer.zero_grad()

            total_loss += loss.item()

        avg_loss = total_loss / len(dataloader)

        # Log metrics
        logger.log_metric(step=epoch, split='train', name='loss', value=avg_loss)
        logger.info(f"Epoch {epoch}/{config.training.epochs}: loss={avg_loss:.4f}")

    logger.info("Training complete!")
    logger.info(f"Logs saved to: {logger.run_dir}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

docs.title: Step 3: Run Your Experiment
docs.text:
Run with default config:

python train.py config.yaml

Run with parameter overrides:

python train.py config.yaml training.lr=0.01 training.epochs=20

Add new parameters:

python train.py config.yaml +optimizer.weight_decay=0.0001

docs.title: Step 4: Check Your Results
docs.text:
ExpMate automatically creates a run directory with the following structure:

runs/
└── exp_20250123_143022/
    ├── run.yaml              # Full config used for this run
    ├── exp.log               # Human-readable logs
    ├── events.jsonl          # Machine-readable event logs
    ├── metrics.csv           # All logged metrics
    └── best.json             # Best metric values

docs.title: Step 5: Compare Experiments
docs.text:
Run multiple experiments with different hyperparameters:

python train.py config.yaml training.lr=0.001
python train.py config.yaml training.lr=0.01
python train.py config.yaml training.lr=0.1

Compare them using the CLI:

expmate compare runs/exp_*

Visualize training curves:

expmate viz runs/exp_* --metrics loss

docs.title: What's Next?
docs.text:
• Learn about Basic Concepts
  • Explore Configuration Management
  • Check out Complete Examples

docs.title: Checkpoint Management
docs.text:
(Documentation in progress - will be added in next update)

For now, see the API Reference and examples.

docs.title: CLI Tools
docs.text: ExpMate provides command-line tools for experiment management and analysis.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
CLI tools are available after installing ExpMate:

pip install expmate[viz]  # For visualization features

Verify installation:

expmate --help

docs.title: Compare Runs
docs.text: Compare multiple experiment runs and their metrics.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
# Compare all runs in a directory
expmate compare runs/exp_*

# Compare specific runs
expmate compare runs/exp1 runs/exp2 runs/exp3

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
# Compare specific metrics
expmate compare runs/exp_* --metrics loss accuracy

# Show configuration differences
expmate compare runs/exp_* --show-config

# Show git information
expmate compare runs/exp_* --show-git

# Export to CSV
expmate compare runs/exp_* --output results.csv

# Pretty print table
expmate compare runs/exp_* --format table

docs.title: Visualize Metrics
docs.text: Plot training curves and compare experiments.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
# Plot metrics for a single run
expmate viz runs/exp1

# Plot specific metrics
expmate viz runs/exp1 --metrics loss accuracy

# Compare multiple runs
expmate viz runs/exp1 runs/exp2 runs/exp3 --metrics loss

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
# Specify output file
expmate viz runs/exp1 --output metrics.png

# Choose plot style
expmate viz runs/exp1 --style seaborn
expmate viz runs/exp1 --style ggplot

# Filter by split
expmate viz runs/exp1 --split train
expmate viz runs/exp1 --split val

# Smooth curves
expmate viz runs/exp1 --smooth 0.9

# Set axis limits
expmate viz runs/exp1 --ylim 0 1

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
# Create comparison plot
expmate viz \
    runs/exp_lr_0.001 \
    runs/exp_lr_0.01 \
    runs/exp_lr_0.1 \
    --metrics loss accuracy \
    --style seaborn \
    --output learning_rate_comparison.png

docs.title: Hyperparameter Sweeps
docs.text: Run systematic hyperparameter searches.

docs.title: Grid Search
docs.text:
expmate sweep config.yaml \
    --param training.lr 0.001 0.01 0.1 \
    --param model.hidden_dim 128 256 512 \
    --script train.py \
    --runs-dir sweeps/grid

This creates 3 × 3 = 9 runs with all combinations.

docs.title: Random Search
docs.text:
expmate sweep config.yaml \
    --param training.lr 0.001:0.1:log \
    --param training.dropout 0.1:0.5 \
    --param model.hidden_dim 128:512:int \
    --num-samples 20 \
    --mode random \
    --script train.py

Parameter syntax: - min:max: Uniform distribution - min:max:log: Log-uniform distribution - min:max:int: Integer uniform distribution

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
# Parallel execution
expmate sweep config.yaml \
    --param training.lr 0.001 0.01 0.1 \
    --script train.py \
    --parallel 4  # Run 4 experiments in parallel

# GPU assignment
expmate sweep config.yaml \
    --param training.lr 0.001 0.01 0.1 \
    --script train.py \
    --gpus 0,1,2,3  # Distribute across GPUs

# Resume interrupted sweep
expmate sweep config.yaml \
    --param training.lr 0.001 0.01 0.1 \
    --script train.py \
    --resume sweeps/grid

# Dry run (print commands without executing)
expmate sweep config.yaml \
    --param training.lr 0.001 0.01 0.1 \
    --script train.py \
    --dry-run

docs.title: Example Workflow
docs.text:
# 1. Run hyperparameter sweep
expmate sweep config.yaml \
    --param training.lr 0.0001 0.001 0.01 \
    --param training.weight_decay 0 0.0001 0.001 \
    --script train.py \
    --runs-dir sweeps/lr_wd \
    --parallel 3

# 2. Compare results
expmate compare sweeps/lr_wd/exp_* --output sweep_results.csv

# 3. Visualize best runs
expmate viz \
    sweeps/lr_wd/exp_001 \
    sweeps/lr_wd/exp_004 \
    sweeps/lr_wd/exp_007 \
    --metrics val_loss val_accuracy

docs.title: Python API
docs.text: You can also use CLI functionality from Python:

docs.title: Compare Runs
docs.text:
from expmate.cli.compare import compare_runs

df = compare_runs(
    run_dirs=['runs/exp1', 'runs/exp2', 'runs/exp3'],
    metrics=['loss', 'accuracy'],
    show_config=True
)
print(df)

docs.title: Visualize Metrics
docs.text:
from expmate.cli.visualize import plot_metrics

plot_metrics(
    run_dirs=['runs/exp1', 'runs/exp2'],
    metrics=['loss', 'accuracy'],
    output_file='comparison.png',
    style='seaborn'
)

docs.title: Generate Sweep
docs.text:
from expmate.cli.sweep import generate_sweep_configs, run_sweep

# Generate configurations
configs = generate_sweep_configs(
    base_config_file='config.yaml',
    param_grid={
        'training.lr': [0.001, 0.01, 0.1],
        'model.hidden_dim': [128, 256, 512]
    }
)

# Run sweep
run_sweep(
    script='train.py',
    configs=configs,
    runs_dir='sweeps/grid',
    parallel=4
)

docs.title: Custom Analysis Scripts
docs.text:
Combine CLI tools with custom analysis:

import pandas as pd
from expmate.cli.compare import load_run_info

# Load all runs
runs = [load_run_info(f'runs/{d}') for d in os.listdir('runs')]

# Filter runs by config
high_lr_runs = [r for r in runs if r['config']['training']['lr'] > 0.01]

# Analyze
df = pd.DataFrame([{
    'run_id': r['run_id'],
    'lr': r['config']['training']['lr'],
    'best_loss': r['best_metrics']['loss']['value']
} for r in high_lr_runs])

print(df.sort_values('best_loss'))

docs.title: Integration with Other Tools
docs.text:
# Export to pandas for analysis
expmate compare runs/exp_* --output results.csv
python analyze.py results.csv

# Generate plots for reports
expmate viz runs/exp_* --output figure1.png
expmate viz runs/exp_* --metrics accuracy --output figure2.png

# Create summary report
expmate compare runs/exp_* > results.txt
cat results.txt

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
1. Use glob patterns to match multiple runs: runs/exp_*
  2. Export to CSV for custom analysis: --output results.csv
  3. Run sweeps in parallel to save time: --parallel 4
  4. Use dry run to verify commands: --dry-run
  5. Combine tools for comprehensive analysis

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Configuration Management
  • Experiment Logging
  • Examples: Hyperparameter Sweeps

docs.title: Configuration Management
docs.text: ExpMate provides a powerful configuration system for managing experiment parameters.

docs.title: From YAML File
docs.text:
from expmate.config import load_config

config = load_config('config.yaml')

docs.title: With Overrides
docs.text:
config = load_config(
    'config.yaml',
    overrides=['training.lr=0.01', 'model.hidden_dim=512']
)

docs.title: From Dictionary
docs.text:
config = load_config({
    'model': {'hidden_dim': 256},
    'training': {'lr': 0.001}
})

docs.title: Multiple Config Files
docs.text:
Merge multiple config files (later files override earlier ones):

config = load_config(['base.yaml', 'experiment.yaml'])

docs.title: Using parse_config
docs.text:
The simplest way to parse configs with CLI overrides:

from expmate import parse_config

# Automatically parses first argument as config file
# and remaining arguments as overrides
config = parse_config()

Usage:

python train.py config.yaml training.lr=0.01

docs.title: Using ConfigArgumentParser
docs.text:
For more control, use the argument parser directly:

from expmate import ConfigArgumentParser

parser = ConfigArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument('--config', type=str, required=True)
parser.add_argument('--gpu', type=int, default=0)
config = parser.parse_config()

Usage:

python train.py --config config.yaml --gpu 2 training.lr=0.01

docs.title: Basic Override
docs.text: python train.py config.yaml key=value

docs.title: Nested Values
docs.text:
Use dot notation for nested values:

python train.py config.yaml model.hidden_dim=512
python train.py config.yaml training.optimizer.lr=0.01

docs.title: Adding New Keys
docs.text:
Add new keys with the + prefix:

python train.py config.yaml +optimizer.weight_decay=0.0001

Without +, overriding a non-existent key will raise an error.

docs.title: Type Hints
docs.text:
Use type hints for ambiguous values:

# Force float type (useful for scientific notation)
python train.py config.yaml training.lr:float=1e-3

# Force int type
python train.py config.yaml model.layers:int=10

# Force string type
python train.py config.yaml model.name:str=resnet

docs.title: Lists and Complex Values
docs.text:
Use JSON syntax for lists and dictionaries:

# Lists
python train.py config.yaml model.layers='[64,128,256]'

# Dictionaries
python train.py config.yaml optimizer='{"name":"adam","lr":0.001}'

docs.title: Timestamp Variables
docs.text:
run_id: "exp_${now:%Y%m%d_%H%M%S}"  # exp_20250123_143022
log_dir: "logs_${now:%Y%m%d}"        # logs_20250123

Format codes follow Python's strftime: - %Y: 4-digit year (2025) - %m: Month (01-12) - %d: Day (01-31) - %H: Hour (00-23) - %M: Minute (00-59) - %S: Second (00-59)

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
data_dir: "${DATA_ROOT}/train"
cache_dir: "${HOME}/.cache/expmate"

docs.title: Config References
docs.text:
Reference other config values:

model:
  hidden_dim: 256

training:
  batch_size: 32

# Reference other values
model_name: "model_h${model.hidden_dim}"  # model_h256
output_dir: "outputs/${run_id}"

docs.title: Hostname Variables
docs.text: run_id: "exp_${hostname}_${now:%Y%m%d_%H%M%S}"

docs.title: Access Patterns
docs.text:
The Config object supports multiple access patterns:

from expmate import Config

config = Config({
    'model': {'hidden_dim': 256},
    'training': {'lr': 0.001}
})

# Dictionary-style (supports any key)
print(config['model']['hidden_dim'])  # 256

# Attribute-style (preferred, cleaner syntax)
print(config.model.hidden_dim)  # 256
print(config.training.lr)  # 0.001

# Get with default
print(config.get('missing_key', 'default'))  # 'default'

docs.title: Conversion Methods
docs.text:
# Convert to dictionary
config_dict = config.to_dict()

# Convert to flat dictionary
flat_dict = config.to_flat_dict()
# {'model.hidden_dim': 256, 'training.lr': 0.001}

# Convert to YAML string
yaml_str = config.to_yaml()

docs.title: Updating Config
docs.text:
# Update single value
config.model.hidden_dim = 512

# Update multiple values
config.update({'training': {'lr': 0.01, 'epochs': 100}})

# Merge with another config
config.merge(other_config)

docs.title: Save to YAML
docs.text: config.save('config.yaml')

docs.title: Save Run Config
docs.text:
ExpMate automatically saves the full config to the run directory:

from expmate import ExperimentLogger

logger = ExperimentLogger(run_dir=f"runs/{config.run_id}")
# Automatically saves config to runs/{run_id}/run.yaml

docs.title: Config Validation
docs.text:
from expmate.config import Config

config = Config(data)

# Check required keys
assert 'model' in config
assert 'training' in config

# Validate types
assert isinstance(config.training.lr, float)
assert isinstance(config.training.epochs, int)

docs.title: Deep Merging
docs.text:
When loading multiple config files, nested dictionaries are merged deeply:

# base.yaml
model:
  type: resnet
  hidden_dim: 256

# experiment.yaml
model:
  hidden_dim: 512
  dropout: 0.1

Result: 

config = load_config(['base.yaml', 'experiment.yaml'])
# config.model = {'type': 'resnet', 'hidden_dim': 512, 'dropout': 0.1}

docs.title: Type Preservation
docs.text:
ExpMate preserves types when overriding values:

# config.yaml
training:
  lr: 0.001  # float
  epochs: 10  # int

# Type is preserved
python train.py config.yaml training.lr=0.01  # Stays float
python train.py config.yaml training.epochs=20  # Stays int

# Override with string (automatic type detection)
python train.py config.yaml training.lr=1e-3  # Parsed as float

docs.title: 1. Use Hierarchical Organization
docs.text:
# Good: organized by component
model:
  type: resnet
  hidden_dim: 256

data:
  path: /data
  batch_size: 32

training:
  epochs: 100
  lr: 0.001

docs.title: 2. Set Sensible Defaults
docs.text:
# Include reasonable defaults for all parameters
seed: 42
device: cuda

model:
  type: resnet
  hidden_dim: 256
  dropout: 0.1

docs.title: 3. Use Descriptive Names
docs.text:
# Good
training:
  learning_rate: 0.001
  num_epochs: 100

# Avoid
training:
  lr: 0.001
  n: 100

docs.title: 4. Document Your Config
docs.text:
# Model configuration
model:
  type: resnet  # Options: resnet, vgg, densenet
  hidden_dim: 256  # Hidden layer dimension
  dropout: 0.1  # Dropout probability (0.0 - 1.0)

docs.title: 5. Use Variable Interpolation
docs.text:
# Reference common values
run_id: "exp_${now:%Y%m%d_%H%M%S}"
output_dir: "outputs/${run_id}"
checkpoint_dir: "${output_dir}/checkpoints"

docs.title: Example: Complete Config File
docs.text:
# Experiment identification
run_id: "exp_${now:%Y%m%d_%H%M%S}"
seed: 42
device: cuda

# Model architecture
model:
  type: resnet50
  pretrained: true
  num_classes: 10
  hidden_dim: 256
  dropout: 0.1

# Dataset configuration
data:
  root: ${DATA_ROOT}/imagenet
  train_split: train
  val_split: val
  batch_size: 32
  num_workers: 4
  augmentation:
    horizontal_flip: true
    random_crop: true
    normalize: true

# Training configuration
training:
  epochs: 100
  lr: 0.001
  weight_decay: 0.0001
  momentum: 0.9
  scheduler:
    type: cosine
    warmup_epochs: 10
  gradient_clip: 1.0

# Logging configuration
logging:
  interval: 100
  checkpoint_interval: 5
  track_best:
    - name: val_loss
      mode: min
    - name: val_accuracy
      mode: max

# Experiment tracking
wandb:
  enabled: true
  project: my-project
  entity: my-team

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Quick Start
  • Basic Concepts
  • API Reference: Config

docs.title: Distributed Training
docs.text:
(Documentation in progress - will be added in next update)

For now, see the DDP Example and examples.

docs.title: Experiment Logging
docs.text: ExpMate provides a structured logging system for tracking experiments.

docs.title: ExperimentLogger
docs.text: The ExperimentLogger class is the main interface for logging.

docs.title: Basic Setup
docs.text:
from expmate import ExperimentLogger

logger = ExperimentLogger(
    run_dir='runs/exp1',
    rank=0,              # Process rank (for distributed training)
    run_id='exp1',       # Unique run identifier
    log_level='INFO',    # Logging level
    console_output=True  # Print to console
)

docs.title: Level-Specific Methods (Preferred)
docs.text:
logger.info("Training started")
logger.debug("Batch processing time: 0.5s")
logger.warning("Learning rate might be too high")
logger.error("NaN detected in loss")

docs.title: Generic Method
docs.text:
logger.log("Training started", level="INFO")
logger.log("Debug message", level="DEBUG")

docs.title: Log Levels
docs.text:
• DEBUG: Detailed diagnostic information
  • INFO: General information about progress
  • WARNING: Warning messages for potential issues
  • ERROR: Error messages

docs.title: Log Individual Metrics
docs.text:
logger.log_metric(
    step=epoch,         # Training step/epoch
    split='train',      # 'train', 'val', 'test', etc.
    name='loss',        # Metric name
    value=0.5          # Metric value
)

logger.log_metric(step=epoch, split='train', name='accuracy', value=0.95)
logger.log_metric(step=epoch, split='val', name='loss', value=0.4)

docs.title: Log Multiple Metrics
docs.text:
# Log several metrics at once
for name, value in metrics.items():
    logger.log_metric(step=epoch, split='train', name=name, value=value)

docs.title: Best Metrics Tracking
docs.text:
Track and save the best values for important metrics:

# Configure tracking
logger.track_best('val_loss', mode='min')      # Lower is better
logger.track_best('val_accuracy', mode='max')  # Higher is better

# Log metrics normally
logger.log_metric(step=epoch, split='val', name='loss', value=0.5)
logger.log_metric(step=epoch, split='val', name='accuracy', value=0.95)

# Best values are automatically tracked and saved to best.json

Access best metrics:

best_loss = logger.best_metrics['val_loss']
print(f"Best loss: {best_loss['value']} at step {best_loss['step']}")

docs.title: Profiling
docs.text:
Profile code sections to measure execution time:

# Context manager
with logger.profile('data_loading'):
    data = load_data()

with logger.profile('forward_pass'):
    output = model(input)

# Profiling results are logged automatically

docs.title: Log Files
docs.text: ExpMate creates several log files automatically:

docs.title: Machine-Readable Events (events.jsonl)
docs.text:
{"timestamp": "2025-01-23T14:30:22", "level": "INFO", "message": "Starting experiment"}
{"timestamp": "2025-01-23T14:30:23", "level": "INFO", "message": "Epoch 0/10: loss=0.5234"}

docs.title: Metrics CSV (metrics.csv)
docs.text:
step,split,name,value,wall_time
0,train,loss,0.5234,1706017822
1,train,loss,0.4567,1706017823
0,val,loss,0.4123,1706017822

docs.title: Best Metrics (best.json)
docs.text:
{
  "val_loss": {
    "value": 0.4123,
    "step": 5,
    "mode": "min"
  },
  "val_accuracy": {
    "value": 0.9567,
    "step": 8,
    "mode": "max"
  }
}

docs.title: Distributed Training
docs.text:
ExpMate supports rank-aware logging for distributed training:

from expmate.torch import mp

# Setup DDP
rank, local_rank, world_size = mp.setup_ddp()

# Create logger with rank
logger = ExperimentLogger(
    run_dir=run_dir,
    rank=rank  # Each process has its own rank
)

# Rank 0 writes to main log files
# Other ranks write to separate files (exp_rank1.log, etc.)
if rank == 0:
    logger.info(f"Training on {world_size} GPUs")

# All ranks can log
logger.info(f"Rank {rank}: Processing batch")

docs.title: Log Files in DDP
docs.text:
runs/exp1/
├── exp.log                 # Rank 0 log
├── exp_rank1.log          # Rank 1 log
├── exp_rank2.log          # Rank 2 log
├── events.jsonl           # Rank 0 events
├── events_rank1.jsonl     # Rank 1 events
├── metrics.csv            # Rank 0 metrics (aggregated)
└── best.json              # Rank 0 best metrics

docs.title: Complete Example
docs.text:
from expmate import ExperimentLogger, parse_config

# Parse config
config = parse_config()

# Create logger
logger = ExperimentLogger(run_dir=f"runs/{config.run_id}")
logger.info(f"Starting experiment: {config.run_id}")

# Configure best metric tracking
logger.track_best('val_loss', mode='min')
logger.track_best('val_accuracy', mode='max')

# Training loop
for epoch in range(config.training.epochs):
    # Training phase
    with logger.profile('train_epoch'):
        train_loss, train_acc = train_epoch(model, train_loader)

    logger.log_metric(step=epoch, split='train', name='loss', value=train_loss)
    logger.log_metric(step=epoch, split='train', name='accuracy', value=train_acc)

    # Validation phase
    with logger.profile('val_epoch'):
        val_loss, val_acc = validate(model, val_loader)

    logger.log_metric(step=epoch, split='val', name='loss', value=val_loss)
    logger.log_metric(step=epoch, split='val', name='accuracy', value=val_acc)

    # Log epoch summary
    logger.info(
        f"Epoch {epoch}/{config.training.epochs}: "
        f"train_loss={train_loss:.4f}, val_loss={val_loss:.4f}, "
        f"val_acc={val_acc:.4f}"
    )

# Print best results
logger.info(f"Best validation loss: {logger.best_metrics['val_loss']['value']:.4f}")
logger.info(f"Best validation accuracy: {logger.best_metrics['val_accuracy']['value']:.4f}")
logger.info(f"Logs saved to: {logger.run_dir}")

docs.title: 1. Use Descriptive Metric Names
docs.text:
# Good
logger.log_metric(step=epoch, split='val', name='top1_accuracy', value=acc1)
logger.log_metric(step=epoch, split='val', name='top5_accuracy', value=acc5)

# Avoid
logger.log_metric(step=epoch, split='val', name='acc', value=acc)

docs.title: 2. Log at Appropriate Frequency
docs.text:
# Log every epoch for validation metrics
logger.log_metric(step=epoch, split='val', name='loss', value=val_loss)

# Log every N steps for training metrics
if step % config.log_interval == 0:
    logger.log_metric(step=step, split='train', name='loss', value=loss)

docs.title: 3. Use Consistent Split Names
docs.text:
# Standard splits
logger.log_metric(step=epoch, split='train', name='loss', value=loss)
logger.log_metric(step=epoch, split='val', name='loss', value=loss)
logger.log_metric(step=epoch, split='test', name='loss', value=loss)

docs.title: 4. Track Important Metrics
docs.text:
# Track metrics you care about
logger.track_best('val_loss', mode='min')
logger.track_best('val_f1_score', mode='max')
logger.track_best('val_perplexity', mode='min')

docs.title: 5. Profile Critical Sections
docs.text:
# Profile time-consuming operations
with logger.profile('data_loading'):
    batch = next(dataloader)

with logger.profile('forward_backward'):
    loss = model(batch)
    loss.backward()

with logger.profile('optimizer_step'):
    optimizer.step()

docs.title: Custom Log Formatting
docs.text:
import logging

# Create custom handler with formatter
handler = logging.FileHandler('custom.log')
handler.setFormatter(
    logging.Formatter('%(asctime)s | %(levelname)s | %(message)s')
)
logger.log_handler = handler

docs.title: Reading Logs Programmatically
docs.text:
import json
import pandas as pd

# Read metrics CSV
metrics_df = pd.read_csv('runs/exp1/metrics.csv')
train_loss = metrics_df[
    (metrics_df['split'] == 'train') & 
    (metrics_df['name'] == 'loss')
]

# Read events JSONL
events = []
with open('runs/exp1/events.jsonl') as f:
    for line in f:
        events.append(json.loads(line))

# Read best metrics
with open('runs/exp1/best.json') as f:
    best_metrics = json.load(f)
